items.q: Does 'renewable' mean a resource can never run out?
items.correct: No; renewable resources can still be depleted if used faster than they regenerate, such as overfished stocks.
items.misconception: Yes; renewable resources are unlimited and cannot be exhausted.
items.random_wrong: Renewable only refers to solar power.
items.why: Students equate renewable with infinite, ignoring the need to use within the rate of replenishment.

items.q: What does the 'tragedy of the commons' describe?
items.correct: How shared resources can be overused and degraded when individuals act in their own self-interest.
items.misconception: A natural disaster affecting common land.
items.random_wrong: A law that bans the use of public resources.
items.why: Learners read 'tragedy' literally rather than as the overexploitation of unregulated shared resources.

items.q: Is economic growth always incompatible with sustainability?
items.correct: No; sustainable development seeks growth that meets present needs without harming future generations' resources.
items.misconception: Yes; any economic growth automatically destroys sustainability.
items.random_wrong: Sustainability means stopping all human activity.
items.why: Students assume sustainability forbids all development rather than aiming for balanced, lasting use.

items.q: Which of these is a renewable resource?
items.why: It's Wind energy. “Crude oil” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The area of land and water needed to support a population and absorb its waste is its:
items.why: It's Ecological footprint. “Carrying capacity” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Sustainable management of a fishery aims to harvest fish at a rate that:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Allows the stock to regenerate. “Removes the whole stock quickly” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Sustainable development is best described as meeting present needs while:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Not compromising future generations. “Using all resources at once” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
